topics.title: Per-90 normalization
topics.description: Compare players fairly by adjusting totals to a per-90 minute rate.
topics.summary: Per-90 rates let you compare output when players have different minutes, roles, or match availability.
takeaways: Use per-90 to compare players who played very different minutes.
Pair per-90 with totals so you understand both rate and workload.
Look for supporting context like role and team style before drawing conclusions.

sections.title: Why it matters
body: Raw totals can be misleading when one player has twice the minutes of another.
Per-90 rates normalize outputs to a full-match basis, helping you compare roles and production fairly.

sections.title: How to interpret
body: If a player has high per-90 output but low minutes, look at consistency and recent starts.
If a player has high totals but lower per-90, they may be volume-dependent or play more minutes than peers.

faq.q: Is per-90 always better than totals?
faq.a: Per-90 is best for comparing rates, but totals still matter for understanding workload and durability. Use both together.
There is no fixed rule, but rates stabilize more when minutes are higher. Use context like starts, role, and recent minutes.

topics.title: Efficiency vs volume
topics.description: Balance how often a player shoots with how often those shots turn into goals.
topics.summary: Great output can come from high volume, elite efficiency, or both. Learn to separate them.
takeaways: Volume signals opportunity; efficiency signals conversion quality.
Avoid overvaluing small-sample efficiency without consistent volume.
Compare players within similar roles to avoid misleading extremes.

sections.title: Spotting volume
body: Shots/90 and touches in the box highlight players who generate chances frequently.
High volume without finishing may still be valuable if it keeps pressure on opponents.

sections.title: Reading efficiency
body: Goals per shot and shot accuracy show how well a player converts chances.
Efficiency spikes can be volatile, so check multiple seasons or similar-minute samples.

faq.q: What if a player is low volume but high efficiency?
faq.a: That can indicate elite finishing or a small sample. Look at minutes and role before projecting future output.

topics.title: Reading a comparison
topics.description: Break down head-to-head comparisons without overreacting to one metric.
topics.summary: Use comparison outputs as a learning brief: rate, efficiency, and role context in one view.
takeaways: Start with minutes and per-90 rates to normalize differences.
Check efficiency next, then interpret with team and role context.
Use comparisons for discussion, not prediction.

sections.title: Start with the baseline
body: Always open with minutes played and per-90 outputs before scanning totals.
Comparing like-for-like roles reduces noise in the results.

sections.title: Layer in context
body: Team style, position, and tactical role can change how stats show up.
If two players have similar per-90 rates, context can explain the differences in totals.

faq.q: Can I use comparisons to predict results?
faq.a: No. Comparisons are educational and should be used as a discussion tool, not as a prediction engine.

topics.title: Minutes and role context
topics.description: Understand how playing time and tactical role influence every stat.
topics.summary: Minutes and role determine opportunity. Context tells you if a rate is sustainable.
takeaways: Minutes show reliability and coaching trust.
Role explains why similar players have different outputs.
Context helps avoid unfair comparisons across positions.

sections.title: Why minutes matter
body: High totals can be a product of heavy minutes rather than elite efficiency.
Low minutes may hide upside or reflect role limitations.

sections.title: Role-driven output
body: A pressing winger and a target striker will generate different stat profiles.
Use roles to explain trade-offs instead of declaring one player better.

faq.q: Should I ignore a player with low minutes?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Look for recent trend lines, injuries, or tactical changes that explain the minutes.

topics.title: Building comparison briefs
topics.description: Turn a comparison into a short, shareable insight summary.
topics.summary: Use a structured brief to summarize a comparison for scouting notes or team discussions.
takeaways: Summarize rate, efficiency, and role in three short bullets.
Call out one key advantage and one caution for each player.
Link back to the comparison for transparency.

sections.title: Brief structure
body: Start with a one-line overview of the players being compared.
Add three bullets: rate highlight, efficiency highlight, and role context.

sections.title: Sharing responsibly
body: Always label comparisons as educational and non-predictive.
Include the compare link so others can verify the numbers.

faq.q: Where should I share a brief?
faq.a: Use internal channels or discussions with scouts, analysts, or fans. Avoid presenting it as a prediction or betting advice.

topics.title: Expected goals (xG), explained
topics.description: What xG measures, why it differs from raw goals, and how to use it safely.
topics.summary: xG is a probability score for each shot. Treat it as chance quality, not a prediction.
takeaways: xG quantifies the chance quality of each shot from 0 to 1.
Goals – xG (over/underperformance) is noisier than xG itself; demand a long sample.
Compare xG/90 within similar roles before declaring a striker hot or cold.

sections.title: What xG actually measures
body: Each shot is scored by location, body part, defender pressure, and similar features against a historical baseline.
Summed across a season, xG estimates how many goals an average finisher would have scored from those chances.

sections.title: How to read over/underperformance
body: Players who score above their xG are either elite finishers, lucky, or some mix — single seasons rarely separate the three.
Watch for repeated outperformance across multiple seasons before treating it as skill rather than noise.

sections.title: Common pitfalls
body: Penalties skew xG: separate them when comparing strikers across teams.
Some xG models penalise long-range shots heavily; specialists like deep set-piece takers can look worse than they play.

faq.q: Can xG predict next season's goals?
faq.a: It is one of the better predictors but still leaves a wide error band. Use it alongside minutes, role, team xG creation, and age curves.

faq.q: Why do public xG numbers differ between sites?
faq.a: Different models, training samples, and shot-context features. Always check the methodology before comparing across sources.

topics.title: Decoding position labels
topics.description: How ST, CF, RW, CAM, CDM, FB, and other codes translate into real roles.
topics.summary: Position codes are coarse — the actual role depends on the team’s shape, the manager, and the player’s tendencies.
takeaways: Two players with the same code can play very different roles.
Team shape (4-3-3, 3-5-2, etc.) shifts what each code means.
Cross-reference touch maps and minutes by zone before assuming role similarity.

sections.title: Forwards: ST, CF, SS
body: ST/CF are interchangeable on most data feeds but managers separate them: a target CF holds the line; an ST presses and runs in behind.
SS (second striker) typically drops between lines and is closer to a CAM than a pure forward.

sections.title: Wide players: LW, RW, LM, RM, LWB, RWB
body: LW/RW imply a front-three role; LM/RM imply a mid-block flank; LWB/RWB are wing-backs in a back-five.
Inverted wingers (left-footed RW, right-footed LW) post very different shot maps to traditional wide forwards.

sections.title: Midfield: CAM, CM, DM, BBM
body: CAM is the highest-attacking; DM is the deepest. CM splits attacking and defensive duties.
Box-to-box (BBM) describes coverage rather than depth — expect high distance covered and varied stat lines.

sections.title: Defense and goalkeeper: CB, FB, SW, GK
body: CB profiles split into ball-playing vs aerial dominators; both labelled the same.
FBs vary by overlap frequency: overlapping FBs look more like wingers in attacking stats.

faq.q: Why does the site sometimes list two positions?
faq.a: Source feeds report multiple roles when a player rotates between them. We keep both so peer comparisons span the player’s real footprint.

topics.title: Reading defensive metrics
topics.description: Tackles, interceptions, blocks, recoveries — what they signal and where they mislead.
topics.summary: Defensive counts are noisy because they depend on opportunity. Always normalise by opponent possession and team shape.
takeaways: High tackles can mean elite duelling or simply many duels offered — check tackle success rate.
Possession-adjusted defensive metrics are more comparable across teams.
Defensive output for forwards is often about pressures, not interceptions.

sections.title: Counts vs rates
body: A team that defends deep concedes more chances and tackles per 90; a press-heavy team racks up interceptions higher up the pitch.
Always pair counts with success rate (tackles won / tackles attempted) and team possession share.

sections.title: When low numbers are good
body: Elite reading of the game can lower tackle counts because the player intercepts before a duel is needed.
Look at blocked passes and interceptions, not just tackles.

faq.q: Should I compare tackles per 90 across leagues?
faq.a: Only with caution. Pace and possession styles differ; possession-adjusted defensive metrics are far more transferable.

topics.title: Set-piece contribution
topics.description: Why corners, free kicks, and penalties deserve a separate column.
topics.summary: Set pieces are a stable share of goals and assists — isolating them sharpens every comparison.
takeaways: Penalties skew goals/90; strip them when comparing finishers.
Set-piece assists reward routine architects, not creators in open play.
Defensive set-piece duties show up in aerial duels and clearances.

sections.title: Strip penalties from finishing comparisons
body: A striker with high penalty volume looks more efficient than they are at open-play finishing.
Where data is available, use non-penalty xG and non-penalty goals/90.

sections.title: Open-play vs set-piece creation
body: An assist on a corner is a different skill from an assist after a through-ball. Splitting the two reveals which players truly create chances in flow.
Some teams concentrate set-piece duties on a single specialist — expect a tail in their stat line.

faq.q: How can I tell if a player is the team’s set-piece taker?
faq.a: Check share of corners and free kicks taken. Anything above ~50% suggests they are the primary architect.

topics.title: Form vs trend
topics.description: Tell short-term variance apart from durable signals.
topics.summary: Five-match form is mostly noise. Trends across 20–30 matches reveal real change.
takeaways: Short-term form windows have wide error bars — expect regression toward season-long rates.
A trend needs at least a half-season of consistent change to be credible.
Pair form with role and team-context changes before reading too much in.

sections.title: Why short windows mislead
body: A 3-match scoring run can flip a striker’s xG-overperformance by 0.5 goals per game without any change in process.
Variance is highest for low-volume metrics like goals and assists; rate stats stabilise sooner.

sections.title: What a real trend looks like
body: Sustained shifts in shots/90, touches in box, or pressures are stronger signals than single-match spikes.
Tactical or positional changes (new manager, role change, injury return) are the most common true causes of trends.

faq.q: How many matches before I should adjust expectations?
faq.a: Rates: ~10–15 matches. Goals/assists: closer to 25–30. Always combine with context, not just sample size.
